title: Rope Rescue
setup: Bean bag at top platform (rope/wall side) — the stranded animal
Position yourself between rope grid mid and the top

activity_flow.instruction: Start at rope grid base. Point to the bean bag at the top.
activity_flow.prompt: "There's an animal stuck up there. How do you think you'll get to it?"

activity_flow.instruction: Child climbs the rope grid. Don't lift.
activity_flow.prompt: "Hold the rope with both hands, one foot at a time." Then step back.

activity_flow.instruction: At rope grid mid, child transfers across to ladder mid.
activity_flow.prompt: "What does your body need to do first?" Don't point to the rung.

activity_flow.instruction: Monkey bars across to bean bag at far top platform.
activity_flow.prompt: If they drop: "Your hands got tired. Try again or find another way?"

activity_flow.instruction: Child picks up the bean bag.
activity_flow.prompt: "How are you going to carry it and still hold on?"

activity_flow.instruction: Descend climbing wall grips to climbing wall base.
Finish at climbing wall base. Debrief.
activity_flow.prompt: "What was the hardest part? What did your hands have to do on the bars?"

adult_guidance.headline: Model grip once, then step back
adult_guidance.detail: Wrap your fingers around, both hands on — then move away. Don't re-model mid-climb.

adult_guidance.headline: Silence means planning
adult_guidance.detail: If the child is still and looking, they are working it out. Don't fill the pause.

adult_guidance.headline: The carry is the challenge
adult_guidance.detail: Don't suggest how to hold the bean bag on the way down. The problem-solving is the point.

adult_guidance.headline: Let them find another way
adult_guidance.detail: If monkey bars feel too hard, offer a choice: go back the way they came or try the wall. The rescue still completes.

variations: Two rescues: second bean bag at rope grid mid — collect on the way up or make a second trip
Sleeping creature: don't wake it — child must move slowly and controlled
Pair version: one child up the rope grid, one up the ladder — meet at the top, one crosses for the rescue
free_play: Where should the animal live on the apparatus?
Can you make a route to get it home?
What if there were two to rescue?
why_it_works: The rope grid ascent demands bilateral grip and weight transfer from the first move. The monkey bars are the curriculum peak — both hands working in sequence, grip sustaining load, child self-regulating pace. Carrying the bean bag on descent adds a genuine self-managed constraint: the child must solve a real problem rather than follow a prescribed technique, building confidence through agency rather than instruction.
